name: Kenai River
description:
The Kenai River is Alaska's most famous salmon fishery, flowing 82 miles from Kenai Lake to Cook Inlet. It holds the world record for king salmon at 97 pounds and draws anglers from around the globe during the summer runs.

Two distinct king salmon runs occur in May-June and July, followed by massive sockeye runs that can number in the millions. The river also offers outstanding rainbow trout fishing, particularly in fall. Multiple public boat launches provide access along the highway corridor between Sterling and Kenai.

name: Kachemak Bay
description:
Kachemak Bay is one of Alaska's premier saltwater fishing destinations, located at the southern tip of the Kenai Peninsula near Homer — known as the Halibut Fishing Capital of the World. The bay's nutrient-rich waters support enormous halibut exceeding 300 pounds.

The Homer Spit provides the primary launch point for charter and private boats heading into the bay and beyond to Cook Inlet. Lingcod and various rockfish species round out the bottom fishing, while salmon runs enter nearby streams throughout summer.

name: Resurrection Bay
description:
Resurrection Bay is a deep glacial fjord near Seward that serves as a gateway to some of Alaska's finest saltwater fishing in the Gulf of Alaska. The bay's protected waters make it accessible even when ocean conditions are rough.

The Seward Silver Salmon Derby, held each August, is one of Alaska's longest-running fishing tournaments. The bay produces excellent catches of halibut, lingcod, and rockfish, with multiple species of salmon passing through during summer runs. Seward's small boat harbor provides excellent launch facilities.

name: Prince William Sound
description:
Prince William Sound is a massive inlet of the Gulf of Alaska encompassing roughly 15,000 square miles of protected waterways, glaciers, and islands. The sound offers world-class saltwater fishing in a stunning wilderness setting.

Access points include Valdez, Whittier, and Cordova, each providing boat launches into different sections of the sound. Halibut fishing is exceptional in the deeper channels, while salmon runs flood the numerous streams and rivers feeding into the sound. The protected nature of the sound allows fishing on many days when open ocean conditions are prohibitive.

name: Cook Inlet
description:
Cook Inlet stretches 180 miles from Anchorage to the Gulf of Alaska, separating the Kenai Peninsula from the Alaska mainland. It hosts some of the most extreme tides in North America, with ranges exceeding 30 feet.

The inlet supports major salmon runs that supply both commercial and sport fisheries. King salmon fishing in the upper inlet near the Susitna and Kenai river mouths is legendary. Halibut fishing improves in the lower inlet near Homer. Extreme currents and silty conditions make local knowledge essential for safe navigation.

name: Kodiak Island Waters
description:
Kodiak Island, the second-largest island in the United States, is surrounded by some of Alaska's most productive fishing waters. The island's numerous bays, channels, and river systems support exceptional runs of all five Pacific salmon species.

The town of Kodiak provides boat launch access to the near-island waters where halibut regularly exceed 100 pounds. Road-accessible rivers like the Buskin and Pasagshak offer shore-based salmon fishing, while remote fly-in streams provide wilderness angling for trophy fish.
